steps.title: Apply the Factor Theorem
steps.content: If $(k-x)$ is a factor of the expression, then by the Factor Theorem, the expression must equal 0 when $x = k$.

steps.title: Substitute and solve for n
steps.content:
Set $x = k$ in $-x^2 + \frac{1}{29}nk^2$:
$$-k^2 + \frac{1}{29}nk^2 = 0$$
Factor out $k^2$:
$$k^2\left(-1 + \frac{1}{29}n\right) = 0$$
Since $k \gt 0$, we have $k^2 \neq 0$, so:
$$-1 + \frac{1}{29}n = 0$$
$$\frac{1}{29}n = 1$$
$$n = 29$$
This confirms D is correct.
